summary:
The Great Plague of London (1665–1666 AD) was the last major outbreak of bubonic plague in Britain, killing an estimated 100,000 people — roughly a quarter of London's population at the time — in one of the most devastating epidemic events in English history, dramatically documented by diarists Samuel Pepys and John Evelyn, and immortalized in Daniel Defoe's fictional-historical account A Journal of the Plague Year (written in 1722 but set in 1665). The plague was caused by the bacterium Yersinia pestis, transmitted primarily through the bites of infected fleas carried by rats — the same pathogen that had caused the [[event:95|Black Death]] three centuries earlier. The epidemic began in the spring of 1665 in the poor outer parishes of London, reaching its peak in September 1665 when approximately 7,000 people died per week.

The epidemic's social response was a mixture of medical helplessness, social flight, and superstition: King Charles II and the court evacuated London; those who could afford to leave did so (including the young Isaac Newton, who retreated to his family's Woolsthorpe estate where he developed the calculus and began his work on optics and gravity during the enforced 'annus mirabilis' of 1665–1666). Those who could not leave included the poor and those who tended the sick: the plague doctors with their distinctive beak-shaped masks filled with aromatic herbs; the 'searchers' (often old women) who examined bodies and recorded cause of death; the churchmen who stayed despite the risk. Plague houses were marked with red crosses and 'Lord have mercy upon us.' The [[event:2413|Great Fire of London]] (1666), which followed within a year of the epidemic's end, may have accidentally helped eradicate the plague's urban rat population, though this causal relationship remains debated.
